revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''黄幡神'''（おうはんじん、おうばんしん）は、[[九曜]]の1つである'''羅睺'''を奉ったもので、集落の境や[[村]]の中心、...'
revision.text:
'''黄幡神'''（おうはんじん、おうばんしん）は、[[九曜]]の1つである'''羅睺'''を奉ったもので、集落の境や[[村]]の中心、村内と村外の境界や[[道路|道]]の辻、三叉路などにおもに石碑や[[石像]]の形態で祀られている。
現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる蛇神であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。
==種類== #文字黄幡神 #蛇頭黄幡神 #:[[不動明王]]の様に憤怒を表しており、頭には原型である[[ラーフ]]を表す九頭の蛇が刻まれた黄幡神。 #日月黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)の左右もしくは上下に[[太陽]]と[[月]]を表す文字や絵が刻まれた黄幡神。[[日食]]･[[月食]]を引き起こす羅睺星の性質を表したものとされる。
==関連事項== *[[庚申塔]] *[[九曜]] *[[スサノオ]] *[[ラーフ]] *[[ケートゥ]]
{{Shinto-stub}} [[category:日本の神|おうはんしん]] [[Category:民間信仰|おうはんしん]] [[Category:道祖神|*]] [[en:Chimata-No-Kami]]

'''黄幡神'''（おうはんじん、おうばんしん）は、[[九曜]]の1つである'''羅睺'''を奉ったもので、集落の境や[[村]]の中心、村内と村外の境界や[[道路|道]]の辻、三叉路などにおもに石碑の形態で祀られている。
現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる蛇神であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。
また、吉凶の方位を司る'''[[八将神]]'''(はっしょうじん)の一柱で、別名'''万物の墓の方'''といい、また'''兵乱の神'''ともいう。この方角に向かって土を動かすのは凶だが、弓はじめなど武芸に関することは吉とされている。
習合の結果、神として分類されたが[[仏教]]と深く結びついている神である。
==種類== 見た目は[[道祖神]]と酷似しているが、以下の条件で識別可能。
#文字黄幡神 #:碑石に羅睺(黄幡)に関する[[漢字]]や[[梵語]]が刻まれた黄幡神。 #蛇頭黄幡神 #:[[不動明王]]の様に憤怒を表しており、頭には原型である[[ラーフ]]を表す九頭の蛇が刻まれた黄幡神。 #日月黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)の左右もしくは上下に[[太陽]]と[[月]]を表す文字や絵が刻まれた黄幡神 #:習合した[[スサノオ]]の性質を継承したものといわれる。 #日食(月食)黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)に[[日食]]もしくは[[月蝕]]を表す黒円(円の一部が黒い場合もあり)が刻まれた黄幡神。 #:[[日食]]･[[月食]]を引き起こす羅睺星の性質を表したものとされる #蛇形黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)の姿が完全に[[蛇]]と化している黄幡神。
==関連事項== *[[九曜]] *[[スサノオ]] *[[ラーフ]] *[[ケートゥ]] *[[日本の神の一覧]]
{{Shinto-stub}} [[category:日本の神|おうはんしん]] [[Category:民間信仰|おうはんしん]] [[Category:道祖神|*]] [[en:Chimata-No-Kami]]

'''黄幡神'''（おうはんじん、おうばんしん）は、[[九曜]]の1つである'''羅睺'''(らごう)を奉ったもので、集落の境や[[村]]の中心、村内と村外の境界や[[道路|道]]の辻、三叉路などにおもに石碑の形態で祀られている。
現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる蛇神であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。
また、吉凶の方位を司る'''[[八将神]]'''(はっしょうじん)の一柱で、別名'''万物の墓の方'''といい、また'''兵乱の神'''ともいう。この方角に向かって土を動かすのは凶だが、弓はじめなど武芸に関することは吉とされている。
習合の結果、神として分類されたが[[仏教]]と深く結びついている神である。

#文字黄幡神 #:碑石に羅睺(黄幡)に関する[[漢字]]や[[梵語]]が刻まれた黄幡神。 #蛇頭黄幡神 #:[[不動明王]]の様に憤怒を表しており、頭には原型である[[ラーフ]]を表す九頭の蛇が刻まれた黄幡神。 #日月黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)の左右もしくは上下に[[太陽]]と[[月]]を表す文字や絵が刻まれた黄幡神 #:習合した[[スサノオ]]の性質を継承したものといわれる。 #日食(月食)黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)に[[日食]]もしくは[[月蝕]]を表す黒円(円の一部が黒い場合もあり)が刻まれた黄幡神。 #:[[日食]]･[[月食]]を引き起こす羅睺星の性質を表したものとされる #蛇形黄幡神 #:羅睺(黄幡神)の姿が完全に[[蛇]]と化している黄幡神。 #:原型である[[ラーフ]]を表したものとも、半身である[[ケートゥ]]を表したものであるともいわれる。

現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる蛇神であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。
また、吉凶の方位を司る'''[[八将神]]'''(はっしょうじん)の一柱で、別名'''万物の墓の方'''といい、また'''兵乱の神'''ともいう。この方角に向かって土を動かすのは凶だが、弓はじめなど武芸に関することは吉とされている。

==種類== 見た目は[[道祖神]]と酷似しているが、以下の条件で識別可能。
#'''文字黄幡神''' #:碑石に羅睺(黄幡)に関する[[漢字]]や[[梵語]]が刻まれた黄幡神'''。 #'''蛇頭黄幡神''' #:[[不動明王]]の様に憤怒を表しており、頭には原型である[[ラーフ]]を表す九頭の蛇が刻まれた黄幡神。 #'''日月黄幡神''' #:羅睺(黄幡神)の左右もしくは上下に[[太陽]]と[[月]]を表す文字や絵が刻まれた黄幡神 #:習合した[[スサノオ]]の性質を継承したものといわれる。 #'''日食(月食)黄幡神''' #:羅睺(黄幡神)に[[日食]]もしくは[[月食]]を表す黒円(円の一部が黒い場合もあり)が刻まれた黄幡神。 #:[[日食]]・[[月食]]を引き起こす羅睺星の性質を表したものとされる #'''蛇形黄幡神''' #:羅睺(黄幡神)の姿が完全に[[ヘビ|蛇]]と化している黄幡神。 #:原型である[[ラーフ]]を表したものとも、半身である[[ケートゥ]]を表したものであるともいわれる。 #'''複合型黄幡神''' #:上記の要素が組み合わさった黄幡神。

現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる蛇神であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。

習合の結果、神として分類されたが[[仏教]]、特に[[密教]]と深く結びついている神である。

#'''文字黄幡神''' #:碑石に羅睺（黄幡）に関する[[漢字]]や[[梵語]]が刻まれた黄幡神'''。 #'''蛇頭黄幡神''' #:[[不動明王]]の様に憤怒を表しており、頭には原型である[[ラーフ]]を表す九頭の蛇が刻まれた黄幡神。 #'''日月黄幡神''' #:羅睺（黄幡神）の左右もしくは上下に[[太陽]]と[[月]]を表す文字や絵が刻まれた黄幡神 #:習合した[[スサノオ]]の性質を継承したものといわれる。 #'''日食（月食）黄幡神''' #:羅睺（黄幡神）に[[日食]]もしくは[[月食]]を表す黒円（円の一部が黒い場合もあり）が刻まれた黄幡神。 #:[[日食]]・[[月食]]を引き起こす羅睺星の性質を表したものとされる #'''蛇形黄幡神''' #:羅睺（黄幡神）の姿が完全に[[ヘビ|蛇]]と化している黄幡神。 #:原型である[[ラーフ]]を表したものとも、半身である[[ケートゥ]]を表したものであるともいわれる。 #'''複合型黄幡神''' #:上記の要素が組み合わさった黄幡神。

現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる蛇神であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。
また、吉凶の方位を司る'''[[八将神]]'''（はっしょうじん）の一柱で、別名'''万物の墓の方'''といい、また'''兵乱の神'''ともいう。この方角に向かって土を動かすのは凶だが、武芸に関することは吉とされている。

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おうはんしん}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:East Asian folk beliefs]] [[Category:Dōsojin]] [[Category:Planetary gods]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.

# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おうはんしん}} [['Category:original pages]][[Category:Japanese deities]] [['Category:original pages]][[Category:East Asian folk beliefs]] [['Category:original pages]][[Category:Dōsojin]] [['Category:original pages]][[Category:Planetary gods]] [['Category:original pages]][[Category:Original pages]][[ja:黄幡神]] {{Shinto-stub}}

As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.

== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.

{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

== Related Topics == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.

{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.
== See Also == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.
== See Also == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.
== See Also == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types.

{{出典の明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rāhu (mythology)|ja|ラーフ}}, an {{ill|Asura|ja|アスラ}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} is identified with {{ill|Marici|ja|摩利支天}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Fudō Myō-ō|ja|不動明王}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * {{ill|Nine Celestial Luminaries|ja|九曜}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ}} * {{ill|Rāhu|ja|ラーフ}} * {{ill|Ketu|ja|ケートゥ}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|黄幡神]] '''黄幡神'''（おうばんしん）は、[[九曜]]の1つである'''羅睺'''（らごう）を奉ったもので、集落の境や[[村]]の中心、村内と村外の境界や[[道路|道]]の辻、三叉路などにおもに石碑の形態で祀られている。
現在は[[道祖神]]の様に村の守り神として信仰されているが、元々は[[インド神話]]に登場する[[ラーフ]]と呼ばれる[[アスラ]]であり災害をもたらす神として恐れられた。日本に伝来してからは、日食を引き起こした神である[[スサノオ]]と習合した。
また、吉凶の方位を司る'''[[八将神]]'''（はっしょうじん）の一柱で、別名'''万物の墓の方'''といい、また'''兵乱の神'''ともいう。この方角に向かって土を動かすのは凶だが、武芸に関することは吉とされている。
習合の結果、神として分類されたが[[仏教]]、特に[[密教]]と深く結びついている神である。仏教での[[本地垂迹|本地]]は[[摩利支天|摩利支天王]]とされる。
== 種類 == 見た目は[[道祖神]]と酷似しているが、以下の条件で識別可能。
#'''文字黄幡神''' #:碑石に羅睺（黄幡）に関する[[漢字]]や[[梵語]]が刻まれた黄幡神。 #'''蛇頭黄幡神''' #:[[不動明王]]の様に憤怒を表しており、頭には原型である[[ラーフ]]を表す九頭の蛇が刻まれた黄幡神。 #'''日月黄幡神''' #:羅睺（黄幡神）の左右もしくは上下に[[太陽]]と[[月]]を表す文字や絵が刻まれた黄幡神 #:習合した[[スサノオ]]の性質を継承したものといわれる。 #'''日食（月食）黄幡神''' #:羅睺（黄幡神）に[[日食]]もしくは[[月食]]を表す黒円（円の一部が黒い場合もあり）が刻まれた黄幡神。 #:[[日食]]・[[月食]]を引き起こす羅睺星の性質を表したものとされる #'''蛇形黄幡神''' #:羅睺（黄幡神）の姿が完全に[[ヘビ|蛇]]と化している黄幡神。 #:原型である[[ラーフ]]を表したものとも、半身である[[ケートゥ]]を表したものであるともいわれる。 #'''複合型黄幡神''' #:上記の要素が組み合わさった黄幡神。
== 関連事項 == *[[九曜]] *[[スサノオ]] *[[ラーフ]] *[[ケートゥ]] *[[日本の神の一覧]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おうはんしん}} [[category:日本の神]] [[Category:東アジアの民間信仰]] [[Category:道祖神|*]] [[Category:惑星神]] {{Shinto-stub}}

== 関連事項 == *[[九曜]] *[[スサノオ]] *[[ラーフ]] *[[ケートゥ]] *[[日本の神の一覧]]

{{出典の明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rāhu|qq=}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin originated from [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu (mythology)]], an [[:en:Asura|Asura]] from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. In Buddhism, its [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] is identified with [[:en:Marici (Buddhism)|Marici]].
== Types == Although similar in appearance to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:

After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.

<!--Ōban-shin--> [[Category:Planetary deities]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Liminal deities]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:黃幡神]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11176300]] Wikidata name: Ōbanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{出典の明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rāhu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラーフ|1=Rahu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q950298}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin originated from [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu (mythology)]], an [[:en:Asura|Asura]] from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. In Buddhism, its [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] is identified with [[:en:Marici (Buddhism)|Marici]].
== Types == Although similar in appearance to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, [[:en:Ketu (mythology)|Ketu]]. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] * [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu]] * [[:en:Ketu (mythology)|Ketu]] * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|黄幡神|version=101474151|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11176300]] Wikidata name: Ōbanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rāhu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラーフ|1=Rahu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q950298}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin originated from [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu (mythology)]], an [[:en:Asura|Asura]] from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. In Buddhism, its [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] is identified with [[:en:Marici (Buddhism)|Marici]].
== Types == Although similar in appearance to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, [[:en:Ketu (mythology)|Ketu]]. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] * [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu]] * [[:en:Ketu (mythology)|Ketu]] * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|黄幡神|version=101474151|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11176300]] Wikidata name: Ōbanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rāhu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラーフ|1=Rahu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q950298}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin originated from [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu (mythology)]], an [[:en:Asura|Asura]] from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. In Buddhism, its [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] is identified with [[:en:Marici (Buddhism)|Marici]].
== Types == Although similar in appearance to [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, [[:en:Ketu (mythology)|Ketu]]. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * [[:en:Navagraha|Nine Celestial Luminaries]] * [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[:en:Rahu|Rāhu]] * [[:en:Ketu (mythology)|Ketu]] * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: imported only latest revision from https://archive.org/details/miraheze-wiki-shintowiki-20251218 to avoid excessive disk usage, comment on the revision was Removing {{afc comment}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Navagraha|en|Navagraha|WD=Q1078632|lt=Nine Celestial Luminaries}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rāhu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラーフ|1=Rahu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q950298}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rahu|en|Rahu|WD=Q950298|lt=Rāhu (mythology)}}, an {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|WD=Q624083|lt=Asura}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=honji suijaku}} is identified with {{ill|Marici (Buddhism)|en|Marici (Buddhism)|WD=Q3273446|lt=Marici}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu (mythology)|en|Ketu (mythology)|WD=Q1367259|lt=Ketu}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * {{ill|Navagraha|en|Navagraha|WD=Q1078632|lt=Nine Celestial Luminaries}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|Rahu|en|Rahu|WD=Q950298|lt=Rāhu}} * {{ill|Ketu (mythology)|en|Ketu (mythology)|WD=Q1367259|lt=Ketu}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities}}
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|黄幡神|version=101474151|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism|1=Mikkyō}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=honji suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} is identified with {{ill|Marici (Buddhism)|en|Marici (Buddhism)|WD=Q3273446|lt=Marici|1=Marici (Buddhism)}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin|1=Dōsojin}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu (mythology)|en|Ketu (mythology)|WD=Q1367259|lt=Ketu|1=Ketu (mythology)}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * {{ill|Navagraha|en|Navagraha|WD=Q1078632|lt=Nine Celestial Luminaries|1=Navagraha}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|Rahu|en|Rahu|WD=Q950298|lt=Rāhu|1=Rahu}} * {{ill|Ketu (mythology)|en|Ketu (mythology)|WD=Q1367259|lt=Ketu|1=Ketu (mythology)}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄幡神]][[zh:黃幡神]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2024th year 5月}} [[File:God of Ooban.png|thumb|right|170px|Ōban Shin]] '''Ōban Shin''' (黄幡神, おうばんしん) is a deity worshipped as part of the {{ill|Navagraha|en|Navagraha|WD=Q1078632|lt=Nine Celestial Luminaries|1=Navagraha}} and represents {{ill|Rāhu|ja|羅睺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rāhu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラーフ|1=Rahu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q950298}}. This deity is typically enshrined in the form of stone monuments at village boundaries, within villages, or at the intersections of roads and three-way junctions.
While currently revered as a protective deity akin to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin|1=Dōsojin}}, Ōban Shin originated from {{ill|Rahu|en|Rahu|WD=Q950298|lt=Rāhu (mythology)|1=Rahu}}, an {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|WD=Q624083|lt=Asura|1=Asura}} from Indian mythology, feared as a bringer of disasters. After being introduced to Japan, Rāhu was syncretized with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, a deity associated with causing solar eclipses.
Ōban Shin is also one of the {{ill|Eight Guardian Deities|ja|八将神|1=Eight Directional Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Guardian Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Directional Deities|qq=}}, known as the "Tomb of All Things" or the "God of Warfare." It is considered inauspicious to move soil in the direction of this deity's presence but auspicious for matters related to martial arts.
As a result of syncretism, Ōban Shin was classified as a deity but maintains deep ties with Buddhism, particularly {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism|1=Mikkyō}}. In Buddhism, its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=honji suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} is identified with {{ill|Marici (Buddhism)|en|Marici (Buddhism)|WD=Q3273446|lt=Marici|1=Marici (Buddhism)}}.
== Types == Although similar in appearance to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin|1=Dōsojin}}, Ōban Shin can be identified by the following characteristics:
# '''Inscribed Ōban Shin''' #: A stone monument inscribed with Chinese or Sanskrit characters related to Rāhu (Ōban). # '''Snake-Headed Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with an expression of wrath similar to {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}, often featuring nine snake heads symbolizing Rāhu. # '''Sun and Moon Ōban Shin''' #: Features depictions or inscriptions of the sun and moon on either side or above and below Ōban Shin, reflecting attributes inherited from Susanoo. # '''Solar or Lunar Eclipse Ōban Shin''' #: Depicted with black circles (or partially black circles) symbolizing solar or lunar eclipses, representing Rāhu’s role in causing such phenomena. # '''Serpentine Ōban Shin''' #: Completely depicted as a serpent, representing either Rāhu’s original form or its other half, {{ill|Ketu (mythology)|en|Ketu (mythology)|WD=Q1367259|lt=Ketu|1=Ketu (mythology)}}. # '''Composite Ōban Shin''' #: Combines elements from the above types. == See Also == * {{ill|Navagraha|en|Navagraha|WD=Q1078632|lt=Nine Celestial Luminaries|1=Navagraha}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|Rahu|en|Rahu|WD=Q950298|lt=Rāhu|1=Rahu}} * {{ill|Ketu (mythology)|en|Ketu (mythology)|WD=Q1367259|lt=Ketu|1=Ketu (mythology)}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}}
